keyPoints: Particle Model: Mass is concentrated at a single point, rotational forces and air resistance are ignored
Displacement: s = average velocity × time = ½(u + v)t
Constant Acceleration (Velocity): v = u + at
Constant Acceleration (Displacement 1): s = ut + ½at²
Constant Acceleration (Displacement 2): s = vt - ½at²
Constant Acceleration (Velocity Squared): v² = u² + 2as
Velocity-Time Graphs: Gradient represents acceleration, area under the graph represents displacement
Displacement-Time Graphs: Gradient represents velocity
Newton's First Law: A particle remains at rest or moves with constant velocity unless acted upon by a resultant force
Newton's Second Law: Resultant Force = mass × acceleration (F = ma)
Newton's Third Law: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction
Weight: The gravitational force acting on a mass, W = mg
Normal Reaction: The force acting perpendicular to a surface when an object is in contact with it
Connected Particles: Particles connected by a taut string share the same tension and magnitude of acceleration
Pulleys: A smooth, light pulley ensures tension is constant throughout the string
Resolving Forces: F_x = F cos θ and F_y = F sin θ for a force F at angle θ to the horizontal
Equilibrium Condition: The vector sum of all forces acting on a particle is zero
Limiting Equilibrium: The point at which a particle is just about to move, friction is at its maximum
Frictional Force: Friction acts in the direction opposite to potential or actual motion
Coefficient of Friction: F_max = μR, where μ is the coefficient of friction and R is the normal reaction
Static Friction: F ≤ μR when the particle is not moving
Smooth Surfaces: Frictional force is assumed to be zero (μ = 0)
Inclined Planes: Weight components are mg sin θ parallel to the slope and mg cos θ perpendicular to the slope
Triangle of Forces: Three coplanar forces in equilibrium can be represented by a closed vector triangle
Moment of a Force: Moment = Force × perpendicular distance from the pivot
Direction of Moments: Measured as either clockwise or anticlockwise
Principle of Moments: For a system in equilibrium, total clockwise moments = total anticlockwise moments
Centre of Mass (Uniform Rod): The weight acts exactly at the midpoint of the rod
Non-uniform Rod: The weight acts at the centre of mass, which is not necessarily the geometric centre
Tilting: When a rigid body is on the verge of tilting about a pivot, the reaction at all other supports is zero
Equilibrium of a Rigid Body: Resultant force is zero in all directions AND resultant moment is zero about any point
Couples: Two equal and opposite parallel forces that produce rotation but no translation
Work Done: Work = Force × distance moved in the direction of the force (W = Fs)
Work Done at an Angle: W = Fs cos θ
Kinetic Energy (KE): KE = ½mv², the energy of a moving object
Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE): GPE = mgh, the energy gained by raising a mass through height h
Work-Energy Principle: Net work done on a particle equals its change in kinetic energy
Conservation of Mechanical Energy: If no external forces act, total mechanical energy (KE + GPE) is constant
Work Done Against Friction: Decreases the total mechanical energy of the system
Power: The rate at which work is done, P = Work / time
Driving Power: P = Fv, where F is the driving force and v is the velocity
Momentum: A vector quantity defined as mass × velocity (p = mv)
Impulse of a Force: Impulse = Force × time (I = Ft)
Impulse-Momentum Principle: Impulse = Change in momentum (I = mv - mu)
Conservation of Momentum: Total momentum before collision = Total momentum after collision (m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂)
Coalescing Particles: If two particles stick together after collision, m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = (m₁ + m₂)v
Impact with a Wall: Impulse exerted by the wall = m(v - u), considering vector directions carefully
Newton's Law of Restitution: Speed of separation = e × speed of approach
Perfectly Elastic Collision: Coefficient of restitution e = 1, no kinetic energy is lost
Perfectly Inelastic Collision: Coefficient of restitution e = 0, particles coalesce
Horizontal Component of Velocity: Remains constant throughout motion (u_x = u cos θ)
Vertical Component of Velocity: Subject to constant downward acceleration due to gravity (u_y = u sin θ, a_y = -g)
Horizontal Displacement: x = (u cos θ)t
Vertical Displacement: y = (u sin θ)t - ½gt²
Time of Flight: Found by setting vertical displacement y = 0
Maximum Height: Occurs when the vertical component of velocity is zero
Horizontal Range: The horizontal distance travelled when the projectile returns to its initial height
Equation of Trajectory: y = x tan θ - (gx²)/(2u² cos² θ)
